Here's the architectural point though: stacked PRs are not a UX improvement on top of the existing review model. They're a fundamentally different model. The unit of review becomes the diff, not the branch. Teams that actually adopt this see code review latency drop sixty to eighty percent and average PR size fall below two hundred lines.
B
Because you're reviewing incremental changes instead of complete feature branches. That's... actually a really different cognitive load.
A

GitHub's implementation has rebase-cascade — when you modify one layer of the stack, the whole chain rebases automatically. That's the hard part that third-party tools have been shipping for a decade. GitHub now has it natively.
B
So the question is whether the native implementation is good enough to displace the tools teams have already built workflows around. That's a high bar.
